name: Eros
bio: Eros began this story as what the poets made of him: golden, winged, armed with arrows that turned mortal hearts toward or away from each other at the pleasure of gods who wanted something from the outcome. He served Aphrodite's purposes, as gods of love tend to serve purposes above their own.

What the story does to Eros over its thirty-one chapters is harder to name than what it does to any other god, because it is not a diminishment but a growth, and growth in a god is rare enough to be disorienting. He watches enough mortal love — at Troy, on the road home, at Thermopylae, in the camps of Alexander's army — to understand that what he has been doing with arrows is a very small part of what love actually is. The arrow creates the beginning. It has nothing to say about anything that comes after.

By the story's end, Eros carries the bow still but fires it less, having discovered that love which needs no arrow is the only kind that survives an empire's attempt to rename it. Cupid takes the arrows. Eros keeps the understanding of what they were for. It is, unexpectedly, the better deal.
